pattern: A valid layering into groups 1..m exists iff every connected component is bipartite; within a bipartite component, the widest valid layering starting at some node equals the largest BFS eccentricity (max shortest-path distance) reachable from any single starting node in that component, so try every node as the root and sum the best per component.
hints: If any connected component is not bipartite (a BFS/DFS 2-coloring finds an edge joining two same-colored nodes), no valid grouping exists at all — the whole answer is -1.

Within one bipartite component, fixing a starting node and running BFS from it gives a layering that is automatically valid (adjacent nodes always land in adjacent layers); the number of groups it uses is one plus the farthest BFS distance from that start.

Because the starting node is free to choose, try BFS from every node in the component and keep the layering with the most groups (largest max BFS distance + 1); sum that best value across all components.

constraints.note: each inner pair [a,b], 1<=a,b<=n, a!=b, no repeated edge

edge_inputs.description: single node, no edges — one trivial group
two nodes joined by one edge — bipartite path, two groups
odd cycle of length 3 — not bipartite, impossible
even cycle of length 4 — bipartite, two groups
two disconnected components, one path each — sums across components
star graph — center in its own group, all leaves in the adjacent group regardless of which leaf starts
linear chain of the full 500 nodes — longest possible layering from an endpoint
one bipartite component and one non-bipartite component — the impossible component forces -1 for the whole graph even though the other is fine
bipartite component where starting BFS from the wrong node under-counts groups — must try every node as root to find the true maximum

stress.description: random forest of small trees (always bipartite) built by attaching each new node to an earlier one
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    edges = []
    for v in range(2, n + 1):
        u = rng.randint(1, v - 1)
        edges.append([u, v])
    rng.shuffle(edges)
    return [n, edges]
stress.note: a forest (tree edges only) is always bipartite, so this exercises the depth/BFS logic without ever hitting -1
